story:
As soon as Arlo’s feet hit the wood-chipped trail, he runs. It’s Wednesday, the day he always makes a cake in the mud kitchen. He’ll mix dirt and pond water into the perfect batter. He even brought a stick of chalk for sprinkles. But when he turns into the clearing, he stops hard. A giant tree has fallen, right onto the mud kitchen. The sink is smashed. The counter is cracked in half. He runs back to his mom. He tries to explain, but the words won’t come. “Come! I have to show you.” Mom scoops up his younger brother, Cam, and follows. When they see the wreckage, Arlo insists, “I have to tell the workers!” Mom says they probably know, but they can walk back to the interpretive center later. Cam wails, “No walk!” Arlo thinks about throwing himself on the ground and screaming, but the disaster keeps pulling his eyes. He agrees to tell the workers on the way out.

He inches closer, torn between wanting to cry and needing to know everything about this broken scene. The bark is rough, almost sharp. Twigs and branches litter the ground like a messy puzzle. “I need to clean this up. I need to fix this problem,” he murmurs. He gathers the loose sticks into a pile, then snaps off the smaller branches still attached to the trunk. “It will be easier for the workers to chainsaw the tree if I take the branches off,” he decides. He thinks of Grandpa’s orange chainsaw in the shed, the one that’s loud. “Can Grandpa bring his chainsaw?” he asks. Mom says no—only park workers can do that. The knot in Arlo’s chest tightens. “But I’m supposed to make a cake in the mud kitchen,” he sobs. “Today is Wednesday. That was the plan!”

Mom kneels beside him. Things aren’t going the way he expected. This is a time to be flexible, she says. What if they find another spot? He blurts, “We’ll use the perfectly round stump, wide and level.” Mom points out the good news: the pots and pans survived. The spoons and whisk and measuring cups are safe in the storage bin. The pond is still the pond. Arlo’s fists clench, then loosen. He breathes in and out, trying to steady the pounding in his chest. He scans the Exploration Area. The bench won’t work—grown-ups might want to sit there. The big rock isn’t flat. Panic crawls up again. Then he sees it: a perfectly round stump, wide and level.

He races to the storage bin and selects two bowls, two spoons, a set of measuring cups, a spatula, a whisk, and a circular cake pan. He arranges everything on the stump like a real kitchen. He scoops water from the pond into one bowl and spoons dirt into the other. Little by little, he stirs the dirt into the water, whisking until it becomes smooth, rich mud. When the batter looks just right, he pours it into the cake pan and levels it with the spatula.

By afternoon’s end, Arlo has finished his cake. He makes two batches of muffins and a loaf of banana bread with Cam. He crushes his chalk, mixes it with water, and serves cups of “juice.” When it’s time to go, he leaves the treats on the stump for the next child. The fallen tree and broken mud kitchen almost ruin his day, but they don’t. Arlo finds another way. He is proud of himself for changing the plan and solving the problem.
